children.content: When Shigeru Miyamoto first sketched the character who would become Mario, he worked within extreme constraints. The hardware could render only a handful of pixels per sprite, and every design decision was dictated by what the processor could push to screen at sixty frames per second.

The famous overalls were not an artistic choice. Arms are difficult to animate against a shirt, so overalls meant the body could be a single solid color without losing the illusion of arm movement. The cap solved a similar problem — hair was nearly impossible to animate convincingly at low resolution, and a wide brim gave the face a clear visual anchor regardless of scale.

The moustache replaced a mouth that could not be rendered at the required pixel size. A nose the scale of a thumb made the face read from across a room. Every element of the design was a workaround that accidentally became iconic, each limitation folded into the character until the constraint and the identity were indistinguishable.

Decades later, the character occupies a paradoxical place in visual culture: globally recognizable from a single silhouette, yet built entirely from compromises enforced by hardware that no longer exists. The outline that wraps this paragraph was computed from the alpha channel of a PNG, rasterized into a scanline resistance field, and consumed by a text-layout engine that has no knowledge of who it is or what he means.

The text you are reading wraps around that shape not because the engine knows it is Mario, but because the exclusion assembly describes where the layout field exerts pressure. Hard members hold the strongest exclusion lines. Weighted shoulder bands at lower resistance values create the soft entry and recovery you see at the boundaries of each wrapped line.

This is the principle behind spatial anti-aliasing for collision geometry. The engine does not draw. It asks, for each horizontal band on the page, what resistance does this scanline carry and how far must the line indent? The answer arrives as a numeric interval table, and the answer is the shape.

Miyamoto has described game design as the science of fun — the careful arrangement of rules and feedback loops that produce pleasure without the player noticing the machinery. A character design serves the same purpose. The constraints that produced this figure were not obstacles to the design; they were the design itself. The limitation became the idea.

The wrapping you observe here follows the same logic. The exclusion assembly is not an approximation of the silhouette; it is the only description of the silhouette that the layout engine requires: where may the text not go? Everything else — the image, the colour, the decades of cultural weight — belongs to the rendering layer and is entirely invisible to the packager that placed these lines.
